docs.title: Homelab Infrastructure as Code
docs.text: A complete infrastructure-as-code solution for managing a homelab running on Proxmox VMs. It automates the entire lifecycle from VM template creation to service deployment.

docs.title: What is this project?
docs.text:
This repository provisions and configures a full homelab environment using modern DevOps tooling:

  • VM Template Creation with Packer
  • Infrastructure Provisioning with Terraform
  • Configuration Management with Ansible
  • Service Deployment with Docker Compose

Everything is version-controlled, encrypted with SOPS, and supports multiple environments.

docs.title: Quick Links
docs.text:
• Quick Start - Get up and running
  • Prerequisites - What you need before starting
  • Architecture - How everything fits together
  • FAQ - Frequently asked questions

docs.title: Architecture Overview
docs.text:
graph TD
    A[Proxmox VE] --> B[Packer Templates]
    B --> C[Terraform VMs]
    C --> D[Ansible Configuration]
    D --> E[Docker Services]

docs.title: Technology Stack
docs.text: Component Technology Purpose Virtualization Proxmox VE VM hosting platform VM Templates Packer Automated template creation Infrastructure Terraform VM provisioning Configuration Ansible Service deployment and orchestration Containers Docker Compose Application containerization Reverse Proxy Caddy HTTPS and routing DNS BIND9 Internal split-horizon DNS Monitoring Prometheus + Grafana Metrics and visualization Secrets SOPS + Age Encrypted configuration Automation Task Command orchestration

docs.title: Services
docs.text: Service Domain Description Plex + *arr stack 5am.video Media management and streaming Prometheus + Grafana wil.5am.cloud Infrastructure monitoring Homepage wil.5am.cloud Dashboard Personal Website sfc.al Personal projects Birdle sfc.al Bird identification game Terraria - Game server

docs.title: Getting Help
docs.text:
• Browse the full documentation
  • Report issues on GitHub

docs.title: Concepts
docs.text: Understand the design decisions and architecture behind the homelab.

docs.title: Topics
docs.text:
• Architecture - How Packer, Terraform, Ansible, and Docker work together
  • Network Layout - Split-horizon DNS, domains, and routing
  • Secrets Management - SOPS + Age encryption workflow
  • Multi-Environment - WIL (dev) and NYC (production) environments

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text: An overview of how the homelab infrastructure layers connect.

docs.title: Pipeline
docs.text:
The deployment follows a four-stage pipeline:

graph LR
    A[Packer] -->|VM templates| B[Terraform]
    B -->|Provisioned VMs| C[Ansible]
    C -->|Configured hosts| D[Docker]
  1. Packer builds base VM templates (Ubuntu/Debian) on Proxmox with cloud-init
  2. Terraform provisions VMs from those templates across environments
  3. Ansible configures the VMs, installs Docker, and deploys services
  4. Docker Compose runs all application containers

docs.title: Environments
docs.text: Environment Purpose Location WIL Development and primary Local NYC Production Remote

docs.title: Key Design Decisions
docs.text:
• Docker Compose over Kubernetes for simplicity
  • Split-horizon DNS with BIND9 for internal resolution
  • SOPS + Age for secrets encryption at rest
  • Caddy for automatic HTTPS with minimal configuration
  • Task runner for unified command interface

docs.title: Multi-Environment
docs.text: How the homelab supports multiple deployment environments.

docs.title: Environments
docs.text: Name Purpose Description WIL Development / Primary Main homelab environment NYC Production Remote production deployment External VPS External monitoring (Uptime Kuma)

docs.title: Directory Structure
docs.text:
Each environment has its own configuration under both Ansible and Terraform:

ansible/environments/
├── wil/
│   ├── hosts.ini
│   └── group_vars/
└── external/
    ├── hosts.ini
    └── group_vars/

terraform/environments/
├── wil/
└── nyc/

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
• Ansible uses inventory files (hosts.ini) and group variables per environment
  • Terraform uses separate terraform.tfvars per environment
  • Packer uses environment-specific variable files
  • Shared configuration lives in group_vars/all/

docs.title: Network Layout
docs.text:
The homelab runs a unified networking stack across multiple sites: internal DNS, a reverse proxy with automatic TLS, dynamic DNS for external access, and a VPN mesh connecting everything together.

Tip

For detailed configuration and howto guides, see the Networking infrastructure docs.

docs.title: Domains
docs.text: Domain Environment Purpose 5am.video WIL (primary) Media services (Plex, *arr stack) 5am.cloud WIL (primary) Top-level cloud services wil.5am.cloud WIL (primary) WIL internal infrastructure (monitoring, apps) ext.5am.cloud WIL (primary) External-facing services sfc.al WIL (primary) Personal projects ldn.5am.cloud LDN (primary) LDN internal infrastructure

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
graph TD
    subgraph External
        ExtClient[External Client]
        Cloudflare[Cloudflare DNS]
        DDNS[DDNS Updater]
    end

    subgraph Internal
        IntClient[Internal Client]
        BIND9[BIND9 DNS]
        Caddy[Caddy Reverse Proxy]
        Backend[Backend Service]
    end

    IntClient -->|DNS query| BIND9
    BIND9 -->|"proxied service"| Caddy
    BIND9 -->|"direct service"| Backend
    Caddy -->|reverse proxy| Backend

    ExtClient -->|DNS query| Cloudflare
    Cloudflare -->|public IP| Caddy
    DDNS -->|updates IP| Cloudflare
  • Internal clients query BIND9 for service hostnames. Proxied services resolve to Caddy, which terminates TLS and forwards to backends. Non-proxied services resolve directly to the backend.
  • External clients resolve via Cloudflare DNS and reach Caddy over the public IP, kept current by DDNS.
  • Cross-site communication runs over Tailscale WireGuard tunnels. DNS zone transfers replicate zones between sites so each site can resolve the other's services.

docs.title: Key Concepts
docs.text:
• Split-Horizon DNS — internal and external clients get different answers for the same hostname. See DNS Services.
  • Unified Service Definitions — a single YAML entry per service drives both DNS records and reverse proxy configuration. See Service Definition Reference.
  • Zone Transfers — WIL and LDN replicate each other's DNS zones for cross-site resolution. See Cross-Site Zone Transfers.
  • Wildcard TLS — Caddy obtains one wildcard certificate per domain via Cloudflare DNS-01 challenge. See TLS Certificate Management.

docs.title: Secrets Management
docs.text: How sensitive configuration is encrypted and managed.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The homelab uses SOPS with Age encryption to store secrets alongside code. Encrypted files are committed to Git and decrypted at deploy time by Ansible.

docs.title: Encrypted File Patterns
docs.text: Pattern Purpose **/secrets.sops.yml Ansible group variable secrets terraform/**/*.tfvars Terraform variable files packer/**/*.pkrvars.hcl Packer variable files docker/**/.env Docker environment files

docs.title: Workflow
docs.text:
1. Edit secrets with sops <file> (decrypts in-editor, re-encrypts on save)
  2. Commit the encrypted file to Git
  3. Ansible decrypts at deploy time using the community.sops collection

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text: Everything you need to set up and deploy the homelab from scratch.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The deployment process follows a linear pipeline:

  1. Prerequisites - Install required tools and configure access
  2. Packer - Build VM templates on Proxmox
  3. Terraform - Provision VMs from templates
  4. Ansible - Configure VMs and deploy services

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Quick Start - Jump straight into deployment
  • Prerequisites - Detailed tool and access requirements

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text: Before deploying the homelab infrastructure, ensure you have the following prerequisites in place.

docs.title: Proxmox Host(s)
docs.text:
• Minimum: 1 Proxmox server
  • Recommended: 3+ Proxmox servers for HA
  • CPU: 8+ cores (16+ recommended)
  • RAM: 32GB minimum (64GB+ recommended)
  • Storage: 500GB+ SSD/NVMe
  • Network: 1Gbps+ network connection

docs.title: Resource Allocation
docs.text:
For a minimal dev environment, you'll need:

Component VMs CPU per VM RAM per VM Storage per VM K3s Masters 3 2 cores 4GB 50GB K3s Workers 2 2 cores 4GB 50GB Total 5 10 cores 20GB 250GB

docs.title: On Your Local Machine
docs.text:
1. Docker & Docker Compose 

    # Check if installed
docker --version
docker-compose --version

  2. Git 

    # Check if installed
git --version

  3. SSH Client 

    # Generate SSH key if needed
ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -C "your-email@example.com"

docs.title: On Proxmox
docs.text:
1. Proxmox VE 8.0+
  2. Latest updates installed

  3. Valid subscription or configured no-subscription repository

  4. ISO Images

  5. Ubuntu Server 24.04 LTS ISO uploaded to Proxmox storage

  6. Storage Configuration

  7. At least one storage pool configured (e.g., local-lvm)
  8. Sufficient free space for VMs

docs.title: IP Address Planning
docs.text:
Reserve the following IP ranges for your environment:

Environment Network Purpose IP Range Dev 10.1.20.0/24 K3s Cluster 10.1.20.40-60 Dev 10.1.20.0/24 MetalLB Pool 10.1.20.140-150 Dev 10.1.20.0/24 Virtual IP 10.1.20.222 Prod 10.2.20.0/24 K3s Cluster 10.2.20.40-60 Prod 10.2.20.0/24 MetalLB Pool 10.2.20.140-150 Prod 10.2.20.0/24 Virtual IP 10.2.20.222

docs.title: DNS Configuration
docs.text:
• Configure local DNS to resolve *.local.samuelcalvert.com to your MetalLB IP range
  • Or plan to use /etc/hosts entries for testing

docs.title: Firewall Rules
docs.text:
Ensure the following ports are open between nodes:

Port Protocol Purpose 6443 TCP Kubernetes API 10250 TCP Kubelet API 2379-2380 TCP etcd 8472 UDP Flannel VXLAN 51820-51821 UDP WireGuard (if used)

docs.title: API Token Creation
docs.text:
1. Log into Proxmox web UI
  2. Navigate to Datacenter → Permissions → API Tokens
  3. Create tokens for:
  4. Packer: packer@pve!packer with VM creation permissions
  5. Terraform: terraform@pve!terraform with full VM permissions

docs.title: Required Permissions
docs.text:
For the Packer token: 

VM.Allocate
VM.Clone
VM.Config.CDROM
VM.Config.CPU
VM.Config.Disk
VM.Config.HWType
VM.Config.Memory
VM.Config.Network
VM.Config.Options
VM.PowerMgmt
Datastore.AllocateSpace
Datastore.Audit

For the Terraform token: 

VM.Allocate
VM.Clone
VM.Config.CDROM
VM.Config.CPU
VM.Config.Disk
VM.Config.HWType
VM.Config.Memory
VM.Config.Network
VM.Config.Options
VM.PowerMgmt
VM.Audit
VM.Console
VM.Monitor
Datastore.AllocateSpace
Datastore.Audit

docs.title: Backup Solution
docs.text:
• Configure Proxmox Backup Server or similar
  • Plan backup strategy for VMs and persistent data

docs.title: Monitoring
docs.text:
• Consider setting up external monitoring for Proxmox hosts
  • Plan for log aggregation

docs.title: Security
docs.text:
• Configure Proxmox firewall
  • Set up fail2ban on Proxmox hosts
  • Use strong passwords and API tokens
  • Consider VPN access for management

docs.title: Verification Checklist
docs.text:
Before proceeding, verify:

  • Proxmox is accessible and updated
  • Sufficient resources available
  • Network ranges are free and documented
  • API tokens are created with correct permissions
  • Ubuntu ISO is uploaded to Proxmox storage
  • SSH keys are generated
  • Docker is installed locally
  • Git repository is cloned

Once all prerequisites are met, proceed to the Quick Start Guide.

docs.title: Quick Start Guide
docs.text: This guide will help you deploy the homelab infrastructure from scratch. Follow these steps in order to set up your complete Kubernetes cluster.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The deployment process consists of 6 main steps:

  1. Prerequisites - Set up your environment
  2. Build Execution Environment - Create the tools container
  3. Create VM Templates - Build base images with Packer
  4. Provision VMs - Deploy infrastructure with Terraform
  5. Deploy K3s - Install Kubernetes with Ansible
  6. Bootstrap GitOps - Set up Flux for application management

docs.title: Step 0: Prerequisites
docs.text:
Before starting, ensure you have:

  • A Proxmox server (or cluster) running
  • Docker and Docker Compose installed locally
  • Git installed
  • SSH key pair generated
  • At least 16GB RAM and 200GB storage available

docs.title: Clone the Repository
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/sfcal/homelab.git
cd homelab

docs.title: Generate SSH Keys (if needed)
docs.text: ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -C "your-email@example.com"

docs.title: Step 1: Build Execution Environment
docs.text:
Create a containerized environment with all necessary tools:

# Build the container
cd docker/exe
docker build -t homelab-exe .

# Create an alias for convenience
alias homelab='docker run -it --rm \
  -v "$HOME/.ssh:/home/devops/.ssh" \
  -v "$HOME/.kube:/home/devops/.kube" \
  -v "$PWD:/workspace" \
  -e ENV=dev \
  homelab-exe'

# Test the environment
homelab terraform version
homelab ansible --version

docs.title: Step 2: Create VM Templates with Packer
Configure Packer Variables
docs.text:
cd packer/environments/dev
cp credentials.prod.pkrvars.hcl.example credentials.dev.pkrvars.hcl

# Edit with your Proxmox details
vim credentials.dev.pkrvars.hcl

Example configuration: 

proxmox_api_url = "https://your-proxmox:8006/api2/json"
proxmox_api_token_id = "root@pam!packer"
proxmox_api_token_secret = "your-secret-token"
ssh_password = "temporary-password"

docs.title: Build the Template
docs.text:
cd packer
make build TEMPLATE=base ENV=dev

docs.title: Configure Terraform Variables
docs.text:
cd terraform/environments/dev
cp terraform.tfvars.example terraform.tfvars

# Edit with your configuration
vim terraform.tfvars

docs.title: Deploy Infrastructure
docs.text:
make deploy ENV=dev

This creates: - 3 Master nodes: 10.1.20.51-53 - 2 Worker nodes: 10.1.20.41-42

docs.title: Configure Ansible
docs.text:
Review the inventory and configuration:

cd ansible
cat environments/dev/hosts.ini
vim environments/dev/group_vars/all.yml

Key settings to verify: - apiserver_endpoint: Virtual IP for the cluster - k3s_token: Change from default! - metal_lb_ip_range: LoadBalancer IP range

docs.title: Deploy K3s
docs.text: make deploy-k3s ENV=dev

docs.title: Verify Installation
docs.text:
# Copy kubeconfig
cp kubeconfig ~/.kube/config

# Check cluster
kubectl get nodes
kubectl get pods -A

docs.title: Install Flux CLI
docs.text: curl -s https://fluxcd.io/install.sh | sudo bash

docs.title: Bootstrap Flux
docs.text:
flux bootstrap github \
  --owner=sfcal \
  --repository=homelab \
  --branch=main \
  --path=./kubernetes/cluster/dev \
  --personal

docs.title: Monitor Deployment
docs.text:
# Watch Flux sync
flux get kustomizations

# Check core services
kubectl get pods -n traefik
kubectl get pods -n cert-manager
kubectl get pods -n longhorn-system

docs.title: Step 6: Access Services
docs.text:
Once deployed, access your services:

  • Traefik: https://traefik.local.samuelcalvert.com
  • Grafana: https://grafana.local.samuelcalvert.com
  • Longhorn: https://longhorn.local.samuelcalvert.com

docs.title: Next Steps
docs.text:
• Deploy applications
  • Configure monitoring
  • Set up backups

docs.title: Common Issues
docs.text:
1. Packer build fails: Check Proxmox API credentials and network connectivity
  2. Terraform timeout: Ensure Proxmox has sufficient resources
  3. K3s won't start: Verify network configuration and firewall rules
  4. Flux sync errors: Check GitHub token permissions

For more help, see the FAQ or open an issue on GitHub.

docs.title: Guides
docs.text: Step-by-step instructions for using the services deployed on the homelab.

docs.title: Available Guides
docs.text: • Plex - Media server setup, browsing, playback, and requesting content

docs.title: Infrastructure
docs.text: Reference documentation for each layer of the infrastructure pipeline.

docs.title: Components
docs.text:
• Packer - VM template creation for Proxmox
  • Terraform - VM provisioning and lifecycle management
  • Ansible - Configuration management and service deployment
  • Docker Services - Container definitions and compose templates

docs.title: Ansible
docs.text: Configuration management and service deployment using Ansible.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Ansible configures provisioned VMs and deploys all services. A master playbook (site.yml) orchestrates the full deployment in dependency order.

docs.title: Playbooks
docs.text: Playbook Purpose infrastructure/networking DNS (BIND9), Reverse Proxy (Caddy), DDNS, Tailscale infrastructure/monitoring Prometheus + Grafana infrastructure/external-monitoring Uptime Kuma (VPS) apps/media Plex + *arr media stack apps/games-server Terraria server apps/website Personal website apps/birdle Birdle game

docs.title: Roles
docs.text: Role Purpose common Base system configuration docker_service Docker Compose service deployment tailscale Tailscale client setup

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
task ansible:deploy-all       # Deploy everything
task ansible:deploy-media     # Deploy media stack only
task ansible:deploy-networking # Deploy networking stack (DNS, reverse proxy, Tailscale)
task ansible:ping             # Test connectivity

docs.title: Docker Services
docs.text: All homelab services run as Docker Compose stacks deployed by Ansible.

docs.title: Media Stack
docs.text:
The media stack provides automated media management and streaming:

Service Purpose Plex Media streaming server Sonarr TV show management Radarr Movie management Prowlarr Indexer management Sabnzbd Download client Bazarr Subtitle management Tdarr Media transcoding Pulsarr Request management Frigate Camera/NVR integration

docs.title: Infrastructure Services
docs.text: Service Purpose Caddy Reverse proxy with auto-HTTPS BIND9 Internal DNS server Prometheus Metrics collection Grafana Metrics visualization Homepage Dashboard Tailscale VPN/subnet routing

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text: Ansible uses Jinja2 templates to generate docker-compose.yml files from group variables, then deploys them using the docker_service role. This allows environment-specific configuration while keeping the compose structure consistent.

docs.title: Packer
docs.text: VM template creation for Proxmox using HashiCorp Packer.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Packer builds base VM images that Terraform uses to provision VMs. Templates include cloud-init for automated first-boot configuration.

docs.title: Templates
docs.text: Template Base OS Purpose ubuntu-server-base Ubuntu Server Primary VM template debian-bookworm-base Debian 12 Alternative base template

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text: Templates are defined in HCL files under packer/templates/ with environment-specific variables in packer/environments/.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: task packer:build

docs.title: Terraform
docs.text: VM provisioning on Proxmox using HashiCorp Terraform.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Terraform provisions VMs from Packer-built templates. VMs are defined as a map in terraform.tfvars, making it easy to add or remove machines.

docs.title: Module
docs.text:
The modules/vm/ module handles generic VM provisioning with configurable:

  • CPU, memory, and disk resources
  • Network configuration
  • Cloud-init settings
  • SSH key injection

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
task terraform:deploy    # Apply infrastructure changes
task terraform:destroy   # Tear down infrastructure
task terraform:clean     # Clean up state

docs.title: File Structure
docs.text:
terraform/
├── main.tf
├── variables.tf
├── outputs.tf
├── providers.tf
├── versions.tf
├── modules/vm/
└── environments/
    ├── wil/
    └── nyc/

docs.title: Networking
docs.text:
The networking stack provides DNS resolution, reverse proxying, dynamic DNS, and VPN connectivity across all environments. It deploys as a single Ansible playbook and is the first infrastructure service to run — all other services depend on it.

Deploy networking first

The networking playbook must run before any other service. DNS and reverse proxy availability are prerequisites for CA, NTP, monitoring, and all application deployments.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text:
graph TD
    subgraph External
        CloudflareDNS[Cloudflare DNS]
        DDNS[Cloudflare DDNS]
    end

    subgraph Internal
        Client[Internal Client]
        BIND9[BIND9 DNS]
        Caddy[Caddy Reverse Proxy]
        Backend[Backend Service]
    end

    Client -->|1. DNS query| BIND9
    BIND9 -->|"2a. proxied: true"| Caddy
    BIND9 -->|"2b. proxied: false"| Backend
    Caddy -->|3. reverse proxy| Backend
    CloudflareDNS -->|external clients| Caddy
    DDNS -->|updates public IP| CloudflareDNS

Internal clients query BIND9 for service hostnames. Proxied services resolve to the Caddy reverse proxy IP, which terminates TLS and forwards to backends. Non-proxied services resolve directly to the backend IP. External clients resolve via Cloudflare DNS and reach Caddy over the public IP, kept current by DDNS.

docs.title: Components
docs.text: Component Purpose Docs BIND9 Internal authoritative DNS with split-horizon resolution DNS Services Caddy Reverse proxy with automatic wildcard TLS via Cloudflare Reverse Proxy Tailscale Encrypted WireGuard VPN mesh between sites VPN Cloudflare DDNS Updates public DNS records with current public IP Below

docs.title: Multi-Site Topology
docs.text:
graph LR
    subgraph WIL["WIL (10.2.x.x)"]
        WIL_DNS["BIND9\n10.2.20.53"]
        WIL_Caddy["Caddy\n10.2.20.53"]
        WIL_TS["Tailscale\nSubnet Router"]
    end

    subgraph LDN["LDN (10.3.x.x)"]
        LDN_DNS["BIND9\n10.3.20.53"]
        LDN_Caddy["Caddy\n10.3.20.53"]
        LDN_TS["Tailscale\nSubnet Router"]
    end

    WIL_TS <-->|"Tailscale Tunnel\n100.64.0.0/10"| LDN_TS
    WIL_DNS -->|"zone transfer\n(AXFR)"| LDN_DNS
    LDN_DNS -->|"zone transfer\n(AXFR)"| WIL_DNS

Each environment runs its own networking VM at .53 in the services subnet. WIL is the primary DNS for 5 domains; LDN is primary for ldn.5am.cloud. Each site replicates the other's zones as secondary via AXFR. Tailscale subnet routers provide L3 connectivity between sites over the CGNAT range (100.64.0.0/10).

Environment Network DNS Server Domains (primary) WIL 10.2.0.0/16 10.2.20.53 5am.video, 5am.cloud, wil.5am.cloud, ext.5am.cloud, sfc.al LDN 10.3.0.0/16 10.3.20.53 ldn.5am.cloud

docs.title: IP Address Allocation
docs.text:
Infrastructure VMs use consistent IP suffixes across environments:

Service Suffix WIL IP LDN IP Networking (DNS, Caddy, Tailscale) .53 10.2.20.53 10.3.20.53 Certificate Authority (Step-CA) .9 10.2.20.9 10.3.20.9 NTP Server (Chrony) .123 10.2.20.123 10.3.20.123 Monitoring (Prometheus, Grafana) .30 10.2.20.30 10.3.20.30

docs.title: Service Definition System
docs.text:
A unified YAML service model drives both DNS records and Caddy reverse proxy configuration. Each service is defined once and consumed by both BIND9 zone templates and the Caddyfile template.

graph LR
    A["Per-domain YAML
(5am.video.yml, etc.)"] --> B["_services.yml
aggregation"]
    B --> C["domain.zone.j2
(DNS A records)"]
    B --> D["Caddyfile.j2
(reverse proxy)"]

Service definitions live in ansible/environments/<env>/group_vars/all/proxy/ with one file per domain. The _services.yml file aggregates all domain lists and injects the domain field automatically.

See Reverse Proxy — Service Definition Reference for the full field specification.

docs.title: Deployment
docs.text:
Deploy the full networking stack:

task ansible:deploy-networking ENV=wil

The playbook targets the infra_networking host group and runs four components in order:

  1. BIND9 — DNS server configuration and zone files
  2. Cloudflare DDNS — dynamic DNS updater container
  3. Caddy — reverse proxy with TLS
  4. Tailscale — VPN mesh (via role)

docs.title: Deployment Order
docs.text:
Networking deploys first in the infrastructure stack. The dependency chain:

Networking → Step-CA → NTP → Monitoring → Applications

docs.title: File Locations
docs.text: File Purpose ansible/playbooks/infrastructure/networking/deploy.yml Main playbook ansible/playbooks/infrastructure/networking/tasks/ Per-component task files ansible/playbooks/infrastructure/networking/templates/ Jinja2 config templates ansible/playbooks/infrastructure/networking/handlers/main.yml Service restart handlers ansible/environments/<env>/group_vars/infra_networking/ Per-environment variables ansible/environments/<env>/group_vars/all/proxy/ Service definitions

docs.title: Cloudflare DDNS
docs.text: Cloudflare DDNS runs as a Docker container using the favonia/cloudflare-ddns image in host network mode. It detects the public IP and updates specified Cloudflare DNS records.

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
Edit ansible/environments/<env>/group_vars/infra_networking/ddns.yml:

ddns_domains:
  - "plex.5am.video"
  - "convert.wil.5am.cloud"

docs.title: ddns_domains
docs.text:
FQDNs to keep updated with the current public IP.

Type: list[string]

ddns_domains:
  - "plex.5am.video"

The container runs with minimal privileges (all capabilities dropped, read-only filesystem, no new privileges) and uses the CF_API_TOKEN from SOPS-encrypted secrets for Cloudflare API access.

docs.title: DNS Services (BIND9)
docs.text:
BIND9 provides internal authoritative DNS with split-horizon resolution across all environments. Internal clients resolve service hostnames to local IPs, while external clients resolve via Cloudflare to public IPs.

Tip

For an overview of the full networking stack, see Networking.

docs.title: File Locations
docs.text: File Purpose playbooks/infrastructure/networking/tasks/bind9.yml Installation and configuration task playbooks/infrastructure/networking/templates/named.conf.j2 Main BIND9 configuration playbooks/infrastructure/networking/templates/domain.zone.j2 Per-domain zone file template playbooks/infrastructure/networking/templates/reverse.zone.j2 Reverse DNS zone template environments/<env>/group_vars/infra_networking/bind9.yml Per-environment DNS variables environments/<env>/group_vars/all/vars.yml Domain list (domains variable)

docs.title: Split-Horizon DNS
docs.text:
The DNS server returns different IPs based on where the query originates:

graph TD
    Client[Internal Client] -->|"dig plex.5am.video"| BIND9
    ExtClient[External Client] -->|"dig plex.5am.video"| Cloudflare

    BIND9 -->|"proxied: true"| ProxyIP["reverse_proxy_ip\n(10.2.20.53)"]
    BIND9 -->|"proxied: false"| BackendIP["backend_host\n(direct IP)"]
    Cloudflare -->|"A record"| PublicIP["Public IP\n(via DDNS)"]
  • Proxied services (proxied: true) resolve to reverse_proxy_ip — traffic goes through Caddy for TLS termination and routing
  • Non-proxied services (proxied: false) resolve directly to backend_host — clients connect to the backend IP without Caddy

This logic lives in the domain.zone.j2 template:

{{ service.name }} IN A {{ reverse_proxy_ip if service.proxied else service.backend_host }}

docs.title: Zone Configuration
docs.text:
BIND9 generates zone files from two data sources:

  1. domains — list in group_vars/all/vars.yml defines which zones this server is authoritative for
  2. services — aggregated service list provides A records within each zone

For each domain, the domain.zone.j2 template generates:

  • SOA and NS records with the DNS server as nameserver
  • A records for every enabled service matching that domain
  • Serial number from the current epoch (auto-increments on each deploy)
  • TTL of 300 seconds (5 minutes)
WILLDN 
# environments/wil/group_vars/all/vars.yml
domains:
  - name: "5am.video"
  - name: "5am.cloud"
  - name: "wil.5am.cloud"
  - name: "ext.5am.cloud"
  - name: "sfc.al"

# environments/ldn/group_vars/all/vars.yml
domains:
  - name: "ldn.5am.cloud"

docs.title: Cross-Site Zone Transfers
docs.text:
WIL and LDN replicate each other's zones as secondaries via AXFR. When a zone changes on the primary, it notifies the secondary, which pulls the updated zone.

graph LR
    WIL["WIL DNS (10.2.20.53)\nPrimary: 5am.video, 5am.cloud,\nwil.5am.cloud, ext.5am.cloud, sfc.al"]
    LDN["LDN DNS (10.3.20.53)\nPrimary: ldn.5am.cloud"]

    WIL -->|"AXFR + notify"| LDN
    LDN -->|"AXFR + notify"| WIL
Zone Primary Secondary 5am.video WIL (10.2.20.53) LDN (10.3.20.53) 5am.cloud WIL (10.2.20.53) LDN (10.3.20.53) wil.5am.cloud WIL (10.2.20.53) LDN (10.3.20.53) ext.5am.cloud WIL (10.2.20.53) LDN (10.3.20.53) sfc.al WIL (10.2.20.53) LDN (10.3.20.53) ldn.5am.cloud LDN (10.3.20.53) WIL (10.2.20.53)

Zone transfer configuration is set per-environment:

WILLDN 
# WIL replicates ldn.5am.cloud from LDN
dns_secondary_zones:
  - name: "ldn.5am.cloud"
    masters:
      - "10.3.20.53"

dns_transfer_clients:
  - "10.3.20.53"
  - "100.64.0.0/10"

dns_notify_targets:
  - "10.3.20.53"

# LDN replicates all WIL zones
dns_secondary_zones:
  - name: "5am.video"
    masters:
      - "10.2.20.53"
  - name: "5am.cloud"
    masters:
      - "10.2.20.53"
  - name: "wil.5am.cloud"
    masters:
      - "10.2.20.53"
  - name: "ext.5am.cloud"
    masters:
      - "10.2.20.53"
  - name: "sfc.al"
    masters:
      - "10.2.20.53"

dns_transfer_clients:
  - "10.2.20.53"
  - "100.64.0.0/10"

dns_notify_targets:
  - "10.2.20.53"

Note

Cross-site zone transfers work over Tailscale. The CGNAT range (100.64.0.0/10) is included in both dns_trusted_networks and dns_transfer_clients to allow queries and transfers through the VPN tunnel.

docs.title: Reverse DNS (PTR Records)
docs.text:
Reverse DNS maps IP addresses back to hostnames. Each environment defines a reverse zone for its services subnet and a list of PTR records.

dns_reverse_zone: "20.2.10.in-addr.arpa"

dns_ptr_records:
  - octet: "9"
    hostname: "ca.wil.5am.cloud."
  - octet: "53"
    hostname: "dns.wil.5am.cloud."
  - octet: "123"
    hostname: "time.wil.5am.cloud."

The octet is the last octet of the IP address. The hostname must end with a trailing dot (.).

docs.title: Configuration Reference
docs.text: All variables are set in ansible/environments/<env>/group_vars/infra_networking/bind9.yml.

docs.title: dns_forwarders
docs.text:
Upstream DNS servers for recursive queries. Used when BIND9 cannot resolve a domain from its own zones.

Type: list[string]

Default: ["1.1.1.1", "1.0.0.1"]

dns_forwarders:
  - "1.1.1.1"
  - "1.0.0.1"

docs.title: dns_trusted_networks
docs.text:
Networks allowed to query the DNS server and use recursion. Queries from outside these networks are refused.

Type: list[string]

dns_trusted_networks:
  - localhost
  - localnets
  - 10.2.0.0/16         # local network
  - 10.3.0.0/16         # cross-site network
  - 100.64.0.0/10       # Tailscale CGNAT

docs.title: dns_secondary_zones
docs.text:
Zones to replicate from remote primaries via AXFR. Each entry specifies the zone name and the IP(s) of the primary server(s).

Type: list[object]

dns_secondary_zones:
  - name: "ldn.5am.cloud"
    masters:
      - "10.3.20.53"

docs.title: dns_transfer_clients
docs.text:
IPs or CIDRs allowed to pull AXFR zone transfers from this server.

Type: list[string]

dns_transfer_clients:
  - "10.3.20.53"
  - "100.64.0.0/10"

docs.title: dns_notify_targets
docs.text:
IPs to notify when a zone changes. Must be specific IPs, not CIDRs. Triggers the secondary to pull the updated zone.

Type: list[string]

dns_notify_targets:
  - "10.3.20.53"

docs.title: dns_forward_zones
docs.text:
Zones to forward to external resolvers instead of resolving locally. Accepts either a string (uses default forwarders) or an object with custom forwarders.

Type: list[string | object]

Default: []

# Simple: forward using dns_forwarders
dns_forward_zones:
  - "example.com"

# Custom forwarders
dns_forward_zones:
  - name: "example.com"
    forwarders:
      - "8.8.8.8"

docs.title: reverse_proxy_ip
docs.text:
IP address used for DNS A records of proxied services. This is the IP of the Caddy reverse proxy — typically the networking VM itself.

Type: string

reverse_proxy_ip: "10.2.20.53"

docs.title: dns_reverse_zone
docs.text:
Reverse DNS zone name, derived from the services subnet. Follows the in-addr.arpa convention.

Type: string

dns_reverse_zone: "20.2.10.in-addr.arpa"    # for 10.2.20.x

docs.title: dns_ptr_records
docs.text:
PTR records for reverse DNS lookups. Each entry maps the last IP octet to a fully-qualified hostname.

Type: list[object]

dns_ptr_records:
  - octet: "53"
    hostname: "dns.wil.5am.cloud."

Note

The hostname must end with a trailing dot (.) to be treated as a FQDN.

docs.title: Add a PTR record
docs.text:
1. Edit ansible/environments/<env>/group_vars/infra_networking/bind9.yml

  2. Add an entry to dns_ptr_records:

    dns_ptr_records:
  # ... existing entries
  - octet: "60"
    hostname: "work.wil.5am.cloud."

  3. Deploy:

    task ansible:deploy-networking ENV=wil

docs.title: Add a forward zone
docs.text:
1. Edit ansible/environments/<env>/group_vars/infra_networking/bind9.yml

  2. Add an entry to dns_forward_zones:

    dns_forward_zones:
  - name: "corp.example.com"
    forwarders:
      - "192.168.1.1"

  3. Deploy:

    task ansible:deploy-networking ENV=wil

docs.title: Configure zone transfer to a new secondary
docs.text:
On the primary server's bind9.yml:

  1. Add the secondary IP to dns_transfer_clients
  2. Add the secondary IP to dns_notify_targets

On the secondary server's bind9.yml:

  1. Add zones to dns_secondary_zones with the primary's IP as master
  2. Add the primary IP to dns_transfer_clients (for reciprocal transfers)

Ensure network connectivity between servers (direct or via Tailscale).

docs.title: Verify DNS resolution
docs.text:
# Query a specific DNS server
dig plex.5am.video @10.2.20.53

# Check a PTR record
dig -x 10.2.20.53 @10.2.20.53

# Verify zone transfer
dig AXFR 5am.video @10.2.20.53

docs.title: Reverse Proxy (Caddy)
docs.text:
Caddy serves as the reverse proxy for all web-facing services. It runs as a Docker container with a custom-built image that includes the Cloudflare DNS plugin for automatic wildcard TLS certificates.

Tip

For an overview of the full networking stack, see Networking.

docs.title: File Locations
docs.text: File Purpose playbooks/infrastructure/networking/tasks/caddy.yml Installation and configuration task playbooks/infrastructure/networking/templates/Caddyfile.j2 Reverse proxy configuration playbooks/infrastructure/networking/templates/compose.yaml.j2 Docker Compose service definition playbooks/infrastructure/networking/templates/Dockerfile.j2 Custom xcaddy build with Cloudflare plugin environments/<env>/group_vars/all/proxy/*.yml Per-domain service definitions environments/<env>/group_vars/all/proxy/_services.yml Service aggregation

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text:
The Caddyfile is generated from the unified services list. For each domain, a wildcard server block (*.domain.tld) is created with:

  1. TLS configuration — Cloudflare DNS-01 ACME challenge for wildcard certificates
  2. Per-service matchers — named host matchers (@servicename) route requests to the correct backend
  3. Reverse proxy directives — each service gets a handle block with optional headers, encoding, and transport configuration

Only services with proxied: true and enabled: true (default) generate Caddy entries.

*.5am.video {
    tls { dns cloudflare ... }

    @plex host plex.5am.video
    handle @plex {
        reverse_proxy 10.2.0.5:32400
    }

    @sonarr host sonarr.5am.video
    handle @sonarr {
        reverse_proxy 10.2.0.5:8989
    }
}

docs.title: TLS Certificate Management
docs.text:
Caddy obtains wildcard certificates for each domain using the Cloudflare DNS-01 ACME challenge. This means:

  • No ports need to be publicly open for certificate validation
  • One wildcard cert covers all subdomains per domain (e.g., *.5am.video)
  • Certificates auto-renew before expiry

The custom Docker image is built with xcaddy to include the caddy-dns/cloudflare plugin. Two environment variables authenticate with Cloudflare:

Variable Source Purpose CF_API_TOKEN SOPS-encrypted secrets Cloudflare API token with DNS edit permissions CF_EMAIL SOPS-encrypted secrets Cloudflare account email

The Caddy container exposes ports 80, 443, and 2019 (admin API), and mounts persistent volumes for certificate storage.

docs.title: Service Definition Reference
docs.text: Services are defined in per-domain YAML files under ansible/environments/<env>/group_vars/all/proxy/. Each file defines a list variable (e.g., wil_services, video_services) that is aggregated by _services.yml.

docs.title: name
docs.text:
Subdomain name. Combined with the domain to form the FQDN (e.g., plex becomes plex.5am.video).

Type: string

name: plex

docs.title: backend_host
docs.text:
IP address of the backend service.

Type: string

backend_host: 10.2.0.5

docs.title: backend_port
docs.text:
Port number of the backend service.

Type: integer

backend_port: 32400

docs.title: proxied
docs.text:
Controls both DNS resolution and Caddy proxy behavior:

  • true — DNS resolves to reverse_proxy_ip, Caddy reverse proxies to the backend
  • false — DNS resolves directly to backend_host, no Caddy entry generated

Type: boolean

proxied: true

docs.title: enabled
docs.text:
Set to false to disable both the DNS record and Caddy entry for this service. Useful for temporarily taking a service offline without removing its definition.

Type: boolean

Default: true

enabled: false

docs.title: dns
docs.text:
Controls DNS A record generation. Set to "external" to skip internal A record creation — useful for services that only need Cloudflare DNS records.

Type: string

Default: "internal"

dns: external

docs.title: tls_skip_verify
docs.text:
Skip TLS certificate verification when proxying to the backend. Use when the backend serves HTTPS with a self-signed certificate (e.g., Proxmox, Kasm).

Type: boolean

Default: false

tls_skip_verify: true

docs.title: forward_headers
docs.text:
Add X-Real-IP, X-Forwarded-For, and X-Forwarded-Proto headers to proxied requests. Enable when the backend needs the client's real IP address.

Type: boolean

Default: false

forward_headers: true

docs.title: host_header
docs.text:
Set to "upstream" to override the Host header with the upstream host and port. Required by services that validate the Host header (e.g., Plex).

Type: string

Default: not set

host_header: upstream

docs.title: encode
docs.text:
Enable response encoding. Reduces bandwidth for content-heavy services.

Type: string

Default: not set

encode: gzip

docs.title: read_buffer
docs.text:
Transport read buffer size in bytes. Increase for services with large response headers or streaming payloads.

Type: integer

Default: not set

read_buffer: 8192

docs.title: Minimal service
docs.text:
A basic proxied web application:

- name: tools
  backend_host: 10.2.20.60
  backend_port: 8070
  proxied: true

docs.title: Media service with headers and encoding
docs.text:
Plex requires header forwarding, host header override, gzip encoding, and an increased read buffer:

- name: plex
  backend_host: 10.2.0.5
  backend_port: 32400
  proxied: true
  encode: gzip
  forward_headers: true
  host_header: upstream
  read_buffer: 8192

docs.title: Self-signed backend
docs.text:
Proxmox serves HTTPS with a self-signed certificate:

- name: vm
  backend_host: 10.2.20.7
  backend_port: 8006
  proxied: true
  tls_skip_verify: true

docs.title: DNS-only (non-proxied) service
docs.text:
A service that gets a DNS record pointing directly to its IP, with no Caddy proxy:

- name: hmg
  backend_host: 10.2.20.186
  backend_port: 443
  proxied: false

docs.title: Service Aggregation
docs.text:
The _services.yml file in each environment aggregates all per-domain service lists and injects the domain field:

WILLDN 
services: >-
  {{
    (video_services | default([]) | map('combine', {'domain': '5am.video'}) | list) +
    (cloud_services | default([]) | map('combine', {'domain': '5am.cloud'}) | list) +
    (wil_services | default([]) | map('combine', {'domain': 'wil.5am.cloud'}) | list) +
    (ext_services | default([]) | map('combine', {'domain': 'ext.5am.cloud'}) | list) +
    (sfc_services | default([]) | map('combine', {'domain': 'sfc.al'}) | list)
  }}

services: >-
  {{
    (ldn_services | default([]) | map('combine', {'domain': 'ldn.5am.cloud'}) | list)
  }}

Both BIND9 zone templates and the Caddyfile template consume the resulting services list.

docs.title: Best Practices
docs.text: Scenario Configuration Modern web app with standard HTTP backend proxied: true (no optional fields needed) Backend with self-signed HTTPS (Proxmox, Kasm) Add tls_skip_verify: true Backend needs client IP (analytics, rate limiting) Add forward_headers: true Backend validates Host header (Plex) Add host_header: upstream Bandwidth-heavy streaming service Add encode: gzip Large response headers or websocket streams Add read_buffer: 8192 (or higher) Service only accessible externally via Cloudflare Add dns: external Temporarily take a service offline Set enabled: false

docs.title: Add a new service to an existing domain
docs.text:
1. Open the domain file, e.g., ansible/environments/wil/group_vars/all/proxy/wil.5am.cloud.yml

  2. Add a service entry:

    - name: myapp
  backend_host: 10.2.20.60
  backend_port: 8080
  proxied: true

  3. Deploy:

    task ansible:deploy-networking ENV=wil

  4. Verify:

    # Check DNS
dig myapp.wil.5am.cloud @10.2.20.53

# Check HTTPS
curl -I https://myapp.wil.5am.cloud

docs.title: Add a new domain
docs.text:
1. Add the domain to ansible/environments/<env>/group_vars/all/vars.yml:

    domains:
  # ... existing domains
  - name: "new.5am.cloud"

  2. Create a service file at ansible/environments/<env>/group_vars/all/proxy/new.5am.cloud.yml:

    ---
new_services:
  - name: app
    backend_host: 10.2.20.60
    backend_port: 8080
    proxied: true

  3. Update ansible/environments/<env>/group_vars/all/proxy/_services.yml to include the new list:

    services: >-
  {{
    ... +
    (new_services | default([]) | map('combine', {'domain': 'new.5am.cloud'}) | list)
  }}

  4. Ensure the domain is registered with Cloudflare (required for TLS certificates)

  5. Deploy:

    task ansible:deploy-networking ENV=wil

docs.title: Disable a service temporarily
docs.text:
Set enabled: false on the service entry. This removes both the DNS record and the Caddy proxy entry without deleting the configuration:

- name: myapp
  backend_host: 10.2.20.60
  backend_port: 8080
  proxied: true
  enabled: false

docs.title: VPN (Tailscale)
docs.text:
Tailscale creates an encrypted WireGuard mesh network between geographically distributed environments. Subnet routers on each site's networking VM advertise local subnets, enabling cross-site communication over the CGNAT range (100.64.0.0/10).

Tip

For an overview of the full networking stack, see Networking.

docs.title: File Locations
docs.text: File Purpose roles/tailscale/tasks/main.yml Installation, configuration, and authentication roles/tailscale/defaults/main.yml Default variable values roles/tailscale/handlers/main.yml Service restart handler

docs.title: Deployment Modes
docs.text: Tailscale supports two modes, controlled by tailscale_mode:

docs.title: Client
docs.text: Default mode. Joins the Tailscale mesh as a regular node. Accepts routes from subnet routers to reach other sites.

docs.title: Subnet Router
docs.text:
Advertises local network routes to the mesh. Used on networking VMs to make an entire site reachable from other sites. Enables:

  • IP forwarding — net.ipv4.ip_forward=1 and net.ipv6.conf.all.forwarding=1 via sysctl
  • NAT masquerade — iptables rule for 100.64.0.0/10 traffic so Tailscale clients can reach non-Tailscale hosts on the local network
  • Systemd override — ensures tailscaled starts after the network is online
graph LR
    subgraph WIL["WIL (10.2.0.0/16)"]
        WIL_SR["Subnet Router\n10.2.20.53"]
        WIL_VM["Local VMs"]
    end

    subgraph LDN["LDN (10.3.0.0/16)"]
        LDN_SR["Subnet Router\n10.3.20.53"]
        LDN_VM["Local VMs"]
    end

    WIL_SR <-->|"WireGuard Tunnel\n100.64.0.0/10"| LDN_SR
    WIL_SR --- WIL_VM
    LDN_SR --- LDN_VM
    WIL_VM -.->|"via subnet router"| LDN_VM

docs.title: Configuration Reference
docs.text: All variables are set in the host or group vars for machines running Tailscale. Defaults are defined in ansible/roles/tailscale/defaults/main.yml.

docs.title: tailscale_mode
docs.text:
Deployment mode. "client" joins the mesh as a regular node. "subnet_router" advertises local routes and enables IP forwarding.

Type: string

Default: "client"

Possible values: "client", "subnet_router"

tailscale_mode: "subnet_router"

docs.title: tailscale_auth_key
docs.text:
Authentication key from the Tailscale admin console. Generate under Settings → Keys → Generate auth key. Enable Reusable and Pre-approved for automated deployments.

Type: string

Default: ""

Warning

Store auth keys in SOPS-encrypted secrets files, never in plaintext group vars.

tailscale_auth_key: "{{ tailscale_auth_key_secret }}"

docs.title: tailscale_advertise_routes
docs.text:
CIDR ranges to advertise to the Tailscale mesh. Only used when tailscale_mode is "subnet_router".

Type: list[string]

Default: []

tailscale_advertise_routes:
  - "10.2.0.0/16"

Note

After deploying, routes must be approved in the Tailscale admin console before other nodes can use them.

docs.title: tailscale_accept_dns
docs.text:
Accept DNS configuration from Tailscale. Subnet routers running BIND9 should set this to false to avoid conflicts with the local DNS server.

Type: boolean

Default: true

tailscale_accept_dns: false

docs.title: tailscale_accept_routes
docs.text:
Accept routes advertised by other Tailscale nodes. Client nodes that need to reach other sites should set this to true.

Type: boolean

Default: false

tailscale_accept_routes: true

docs.title: tailscale_hostname
docs.text:
Hostname as it appears in the Tailscale admin console.

Type: string

Default: "{{ inventory_hostname }}"

tailscale_hostname: "wil-networking"

docs.title: tailscale_force_reauth
docs.text:
Force re-authentication on the next Ansible run. Use when the auth key has changed or the node needs to rejoin the mesh.

Type: boolean

Default: false

tailscale_force_reauth: true

docs.title: Cross-Site Connectivity
docs.text: Tailscale subnet routers enable three critical cross-site functions:

docs.title: DNS Zone Transfers
docs.text: WIL and LDN DNS servers replicate zones via AXFR. The BIND9 servers reach each other through Tailscale using their local IPs (e.g., WIL queries LDN at 10.3.20.53), which the subnet routers make routable. See DNS — Cross-Site Zone Transfers.

docs.title: Service Access
docs.text:
Services on one site can be accessed from the other. For example, a client in LDN can reach plex.5am.video because:

  1. LDN BIND9 has 5am.video as a secondary zone (replicated from WIL)
  2. The A record resolves to WIL's reverse_proxy_ip (10.2.20.53)
  3. Tailscale routes traffic to 10.2.20.53 through the WireGuard tunnel

docs.title: Trusted Network Configuration
docs.text:
Both sites include the Tailscale CGNAT range in their BIND9 trusted networks:

dns_trusted_networks:
  - 100.64.0.0/10  # Tailscale CGNAT

This allows DNS queries and zone transfers originating from Tailscale IPs.

docs.title: DNS Integration
docs.text:
Networking VMs run both BIND9 and Tailscale. To prevent Tailscale from overriding the local DNS configuration:

tailscale_accept_dns: false

Without this, Tailscale would push its own DNS servers (MagicDNS), which would conflict with the locally running BIND9 instance.

docs.title: Security
docs.text: Aspect Implementation Encryption All traffic uses WireGuard encryption (always on) Authentication Auth keys stored as SOPS-encrypted secrets NAT traversal Coordinated by Tailscale control plane; no public ports needed IP forwarding Enabled only on subnet routers, not clients DNS isolation Subnet routers disable Tailscale DNS to avoid BIND9 conflicts Access control Tailscale ACLs control which nodes can communicate

docs.title: Set up a new subnet router
docs.text:
1. Set the Tailscale variables in the host's group vars:

    tailscale_mode: "subnet_router"
tailscale_auth_key: "{{ tailscale_auth_key_secret }}"
tailscale_advertise_routes:
  - "10.2.0.0/16"
tailscale_accept_dns: false
tailscale_accept_routes: true

  2. Deploy:

    task ansible:deploy-networking ENV=wil

  3. Approve the advertised routes in the Tailscale admin console

docs.title: Add a new site
docs.text:
1. Set up Tailscale on the new site's networking VM as a subnet router (see above)
  2. On existing sites, ensure tailscale_accept_routes: true so they accept the new site's routes
  3. Add the new site's subnet to dns_trusted_networks on existing DNS servers
  4. Configure zone transfers between the new site and existing sites (see DNS — Configure zone transfer to a new secondary)
  5. Deploy all affected environments

docs.title: Force re-authentication
docs.text:
If a node's auth key expires or the node needs to rejoin:

  1. Set tailscale_force_reauth: true in the host's vars

  2. Deploy:

    task ansible:deploy-networking ENV=wil

  3. Reset tailscale_force_reauth: false after successful authentication to avoid re-authing on every future run

docs.title: Welcome to My Plex Server
docs.text:
You've been invited to stream movies, TV shows, and more -- all for free. This short guide will help you get set up and watching in minutes.

Good to know

Plex is like having your own personal Netflix. Everything on this server has been organized for you -- just sign in, pick something, and press play. No subscriptions or fees required.

docs.title: Where to start
docs.text:
Pick the guide that matches where you are right now:

  • Getting Started -- Create your free Plex account, accept your invitation, and sign in for the first time. Takes about 5 minutes.
  • Browsing & Playing -- Learn how to find movies and shows, use search, manage your watchlist, and control playback.
  • Setting Up Your Devices -- Step-by-step instructions for watching on your TV, phone, tablet, or computer.
  • Requesting Content -- How to request new movies and TV shows by adding them to your Plex watchlist.

docs.title: Is this really free?
docs.text: Yes! You don't need to pay anything. When you create your Plex account, you may see ads for "Plex Pass" -- that's an optional upgrade you do not need. Just skip past it.

docs.title: What can I watch?
docs.text: Everything the server owner has shared with you. This typically includes movies, TV shows, and sometimes music or photos. Browse the library to see what's available.

docs.title: Do I need to download anything?
docs.text: Not necessarily. You can watch in any web browser at app.plex.tv. For the best experience on a TV or mobile device, you'll want the free Plex app -- see the Devices guide.

docs.title: Who do I ask if something isn't working?
docs.text: Reach out to the person who invited you. They manage the server and can help troubleshoot.

docs.title: Browsing & Playing
docs.text: Now that you're signed in, here's how to find something to watch and control playback.

docs.title: Browse the library
docs.text:
On the left side of the screen (or in the menu on mobile), you'll see the shared libraries -- usually labeled something like "Movies" and "TV Shows." Tap or click one to see everything available.

Inside a library, content is displayed as a grid of poster artwork. You can scroll through to see what's available, much like browsing Netflix or any other streaming app.

docs.title: Use search
docs.text:
Look for the magnifying glass icon at the top of the screen. Type the name of a movie, show, or actor, and Plex will show you matching results from the shared server.

Tip

Search looks across all shared libraries at once, so you don't need to pick "Movies" or "TV Shows" first.

docs.title: Sort and filter
docs.text: Inside any library, look for sort and filter options near the top of the page. You can sort by title, year, rating, or date added. Filters let you narrow down by genre, year, or resolution -- helpful when you're not sure what you want.

docs.title: Playing something
docs.text:
1. Click or tap a title to open its detail page. You'll see a description, rating, cast info, and the play button.
  2. Press the big Play button to start watching immediately. For TV shows, you'll see a list of seasons and episodes -- pick the one you want and press play.
  3. Playback controls appear when you move your mouse (or tap the screen on mobile). You'll see familiar buttons: play/pause, skip forward/back, volume, and fullscreen.

Tip

If you stop watching partway through, Plex remembers where you left off. Next time you open that title, just press "Resume" to pick up right where you stopped.

docs.title: Subtitles & audio
docs.text:
During playback, look for a speech bubble icon or settings gear in the playback controls. From there you can:

  • Turn on subtitles if they're available (many titles include English subtitles and sometimes other languages).
  • Switch between audio tracks if multiple languages are provided.

docs.title: Your Watchlist
docs.text:
Found something interesting but not ready to watch it yet? Click the bookmark icon (or the "Add to Watchlist" button) on any title's detail page. You'll find your saved titles later under "Watchlist" in the sidebar.

Think of it like a "watch later" list -- it's personal to your account, so no one else sees it.

docs.title: Continue Watching
docs.text: Plex keeps track of everything you've started. Look for a "Continue Watching" row on the home screen -- it shows titles you haven't finished yet, so you can jump right back in.

docs.title: Playback shortcuts (on a computer)
docs.text: Key Action Space Play or pause F Toggle fullscreen Left / Right Skip back / forward Up / Down Volume up / down M Mute

docs.title: Setting Up Your Devices
docs.text:
You can watch on almost anything -- your TV, phone, tablet, or computer. Find your device below and follow the steps.

Before you start

Make sure you've already created your free Plex account and accepted the server invitation. If you haven't, head to Getting Started first.

docs.title: Computer (any web browser)
docs.text:
This is the easiest way to watch -- no app needed.

  1. Open your web browser (Chrome, Firefox, Safari, Edge -- any will work).
  2. Go to app.plex.tv
  3. Sign in with your Plex account. That's it -- browse and play.

docs.title: iPhone or iPad
docs.text:
1. Open the App Store on your device.
  2. Search for "Plex" and download the app (it's free -- look for the orange play-button icon).
  3. Open the app and sign in with your Plex account.
  4. The shared libraries will appear in the sidebar. Tap one to start browsing.

Tip

Plex may ask you to "activate" the app by going to plex.tv/link on a computer and entering a code. Just follow the on-screen instructions -- it only takes a moment.

docs.title: Android phone or tablet
docs.text:
1. Open the Google Play Store.
  2. Search for "Plex" and install the app.
  3. Open the app and sign in with your Plex account.
  4. The shared server's libraries will appear automatically. Start browsing!

docs.title: Roku
docs.text:
1. From the Roku home screen, go to Streaming Channels (or Search).
  2. Search for "Plex" and select Add Channel.
  3. Open the Plex app. It will show a 4-letter code on your TV screen.
  4. On your phone or computer, go to plex.tv/link, sign in, and type in the code. Your Roku is now connected.

docs.title: Amazon Fire TV / Fire Stick
docs.text:
1. From the Fire TV home screen, go to Find > Search (or use the Alexa voice remote).
  2. Search for "Plex" and select Download.
  3. Open the Plex app. You'll see a 4-letter code.
  4. On your phone or computer, go to plex.tv/link, sign in, and enter the code.

docs.title: Apple TV
docs.text:
1. Open the App Store on your Apple TV.
  2. Search for "Plex" and download the app.
  3. Open Plex. You'll see a code on your TV screen.
  4. Go to plex.tv/link on another device, sign in, and enter the code.

docs.title: Smart TV (Samsung, LG, Vizio, etc.)
docs.text:
1. Open your TV's built-in app store (Samsung: Smart Hub, LG: LG Content Store, Vizio: SmartCast Apps).
  2. Search for "Plex" and install it.
  3. Open the app. You'll see a 4-letter code.
  4. Go to plex.tv/link on your phone or computer, sign in, and enter the code.

Note

Some older smart TVs may not have Plex available in their app store. If that's the case, a Roku or Fire Stick is an affordable way to add Plex to any TV with an HDMI port.

docs.title: Linking any device -- the universal method
docs.text:
Nearly every Plex app on a TV or streaming device uses the same linking process:

  1. Install and open the Plex app on your device. A 4-letter code appears on screen.
  2. On any other device (your phone works great), open a browser and go to plex.tv/link.
  3. Sign in to your Plex account and type in the code. Done -- your device is connected.

Tip

You only need to do this once per device. After linking, the app stays signed in.

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text: Create your free account, accept your invitation, and start watching. The whole process takes about 5 minutes.

docs.title: Step 1 -- Create your free Plex account
docs.text:
1. Open a web browser on your computer, phone, or tablet and go to: plex.tv/sign-up
  2. You can sign up using your email address, or sign in with Google, Facebook, or Apple. Use whichever you're most comfortable with.
  3. Choose a username and password you'll remember. Write them down if it helps!
  4. If Plex asks you about "Plex Pass" or any paid upgrade -- just look for a button that says "Skip" or "Maybe later." You don't need to pay for anything.

Tip

If you used an email address to sign up, check your inbox for a confirmation email from Plex and click the link inside. Check your spam folder if you don't see it.

docs.title: Step 2 -- Accept the server invitation
docs.text:
The server owner will send you a friend invitation through Plex. Here's how to find and accept it:

  1. Check your email for a message from Plex with the subject "You've been invited to share some media in Plex" (or something similar).
  2. Click the "Accept Invitation" button inside the email. This will open Plex in your browser.
  3. If you're already signed in, the server's libraries will appear automatically. If not, sign in with the account you just created.

Don't see an email?

Ask the server owner to re-send the invitation. Make sure they have the same email address you used to create your Plex account. Also check your spam/junk folder.

docs.title: Step 3 -- Sign in and start watching
docs.text:
1. Go to app.plex.tv in any web browser.
  2. Sign in with your account.
  3. Look at the left sidebar. You should see the shared server's libraries listed there -- things like "Movies," "TV Shows," etc. Click any of them to start browsing.

Tip

Plex also has its own free movies and live TV channels. These are separate from the server you've been invited to. The shared libraries from your server owner will have their own section -- look for them in the sidebar.

docs.title: Write this down for later
docs.text:
Keep this info handy so you can always get back in:

Field Value Website app.plex.tv My email ______ My password ______

docs.title: Requesting Content
docs.text: Want to watch something that isn't on the server yet? You can request it directly from Plex -- no extra apps or accounts needed.

docs.title: How it works
docs.text: This server uses Pulsarr to automatically monitor your Plex watchlist. When you add a movie or TV show to your watchlist, Pulsarr detects it and begins downloading it to the server. In most cases, the content will appear in your library within a few minutes to a few hours depending on availability.

docs.title: How to request something
docs.text:
1. Search for the movie or TV show you want in Plex (even if it's not on the server yet, Plex will show results from its online database).
  2. Open the title's detail page.
  3. Click the "Add to Watchlist" button (the bookmark icon).
  4. That's it -- Pulsarr will pick up your request automatically.

Tip

You can manage your watchlist from any Plex app or from app.plex.tv. Anything you add will be detected.

docs.title: What to expect
docs.text:
• Movies are typically available within a few hours of being requested.
  • TV shows will be monitored for new episodes automatically once added.
  • If something isn't available or can't be found, reach out to the server owner.

Note

Not every request can be fulfilled. Some content may not be available due to region restrictions, release timing, or other limitations. If your request doesn't show up after a day or so, just ask the server owner.

docs.title: Removing a request
docs.text: If you change your mind, simply remove the title from your Plex watchlist. You can do this from the title's detail page by clicking the bookmark icon again.

docs.title: Reference
docs.text: Quick-access reference material for day-to-day operations.

docs.title: Topics
docs.text:
• Task Commands - Available Taskfile commands for deployment and management
  • TUI - Terminal UI for interactive homelab management
  • FAQ - Frequently asked questions

docs.title: What is this project?
docs.text: This is a complete Infrastructure as Code (IaC) solution for deploying and managing a Kubernetes homelab. It automates everything from VM creation to application deployment using industry-standard tools like Packer, Terraform, Ansible, and Flux.

docs.title: What do I need to run this?
docs.text: At minimum, you need: - A Proxmox server with at least 32GB RAM and 500GB storage - Docker installed on your local machine - Basic networking knowledge - Patience and willingness to learn!

docs.title: Is this production-ready?
docs.text: While this project uses production-grade tools and practices, it's designed for homelab use. For production environments, you'd want to add: - High availability Proxmox cluster - Proper backup solutions - Security hardening - Monitoring and alerting

docs.title: Packer build fails with "401 Unauthorized"
docs.text: This usually means your Proxmox API token is incorrect. Verify: 1. Token exists in Proxmox 2. Token has the required permissions 3. Token format is correct: user@realm!tokenname

docs.title: Terraform can't find the template
docs.text: Ensure: 1. Packer build completed successfully 2. Template name in Terraform matches what Packer created 3. You're targeting the correct Proxmox node

docs.title: Ansible can't connect to VMs
docs.text: Check: 1. VMs have the correct IP addresses 2. SSH key was properly injected via cloud-init 3. No firewall blocking SSH (port 22) 4. You can manually SSH to the VMs

docs.title: K3s installation hangs
docs.text: Common causes: 1. Network connectivity between nodes 2. Firewall blocking required ports 3. Incorrect apiserver_endpoint configuration 4. DNS resolution issues

docs.title: Can I use different IP ranges?
docs.text: Yes! Update these files: - terraform/environments/dev/main.tf - VM IP configuration - ansible/environments/dev/hosts.ini - Ansible inventory - ansible/environments/dev/group_vars/all.yml - K3s configuration

docs.title: How do I access services from outside?
docs.text: You have several options: 1. Port forwarding on your router to MetalLB IPs 2. Reverse proxy like nginx on your network 3. VPN access to your homelab network 4. Cloudflare Tunnel for secure external access

docs.title: Why can't I resolve *.local.samuelcalvert.com?
docs.text: You need to: 1. Add DNS entries pointing to your MetalLB IP range 2. Or add entries to your /etc/hosts file 3. Or use a tool like dnsmasq for local DNS

docs.title: How much RAM do I really need?
docs.text:
Minimum for dev environment: - 3 masters × 4GB = 12GB - 2 workers × 4GB = 8GB - Total: 20GB for VMs + overhead

Recommended: 32GB+ for comfortable operation

docs.title: Can I run with fewer VMs?
docs.text: Yes, but with limitations: - Single master: No HA, risky for learning - No workers: Workloads run on masters (not recommended) - Minimum recommended: 1 master + 1 worker

docs.title: How much storage will I use?
docs.text:
Base usage: - VM templates: ~5GB each - VMs: ~20-50GB each - Applications: Varies greatly

Plan for at least 250GB for a basic setup.

docs.title: How do I add my own applications?
docs.text:
1. Create manifests in kubernetes/apps/base/
  2. Add environment-specific configs in kubernetes/apps/dev/
  3. Update kustomization.yaml files
  4. Commit and push - Flux will deploy automatically

docs.title: Can I use Helm charts?
docs.text:
Yes! Flux supports Helm. Create a HelmRelease resource:

apiVersion: helm.toolkit.fluxcd.io/v2
kind: HelmRelease
metadata:
  name: my-app
  namespace: default
spec:
  interval: 5m
  chart:
    spec:
      chart: my-chart
      version: '1.0.0'
      sourceRef:
        kind: HelmRepository
        name: my-repo

docs.title: How do I update applications?
docs.text:
For GitOps-managed apps: 1. Update manifests in Git 2. Push changes 3. Flux automatically syncs

For manual updates: 

kubectl set image deployment/app app=image:newtag

docs.title: How do I check Flux status?
docs.text:
# Overall status
flux get all

# Specific resources
flux get kustomizations
flux get helmreleases

# Logs
flux logs --follow

docs.title: Where are the logs?
docs.text:
• Proxmox: /var/log/pve/
  • K3s: journalctl -u k3s
  • Pods: kubectl logs <pod-name>
  • Flux: flux logs

docs.title: How do I reset everything?
docs.text:
To start over:

# Destroy VMs
cd terraform/environments/dev
terraform destroy

# Clean up templates (optional)
# Manually delete from Proxmox UI

# Reset local state
rm -rf terraform/environments/dev/.terraform
rm -f terraform/environments/dev/terraform.tfstate*

docs.title: Should I modify the base files?
docs.text: No, use the environment-specific overrides: - environments/dev/ for development - environments/prod/ for production

docs.title: How do I handle secrets?
docs.text:
1. Never commit secrets to Git
  2. Use Kubernetes secrets
  3. Consider tools like Sealed Secrets or External Secrets
  4. Use .gitignore for sensitive files

docs.title: How do I backup my cluster?
docs.text: Consider: 1. VM level: Proxmox backup/snapshots 2. Application level: Velero for Kubernetes 3. Data level: Application-specific backups 4. GitOps: Your Git repo is your source of truth

docs.title: Where can I get more help?
docs.text:
1. Check the documentation
  2. Search existing issues
  3. Ask in discussions
  4. Open a new issue with details

docs.title: How can I contribute?
docs.text: We welcome contributions! See CONTRIBUTING.md for guidelines.

docs.title: I found a security issue
docs.text: Please report security issues privately to samuel.f.calvert@gmail.com instead of public issues.

docs.title: Task Commands
docs.text: Available commands via the Task runner.

docs.title: Ansible Tasks
docs.text: Command Description task ansible:deploy-all Deploy entire infrastructure task ansible:deploy-networking Deploy networking stack (DNS, reverse proxy) task ansible:deploy-media Deploy media stack task ansible:deploy-monitoring Deploy Prometheus + Grafana task ansible:deploy-games Deploy Terraria server task ansible:deploy-website Deploy personal website task ansible:deploy-birdle Deploy Birdle game task ansible:ping Test connectivity to all hosts task ansible:backup-media Backup media stack configs task ansible:restore-media Restore media stack from backup

docs.title: Terraform Tasks
docs.text: Command Description task terraform:deploy Apply infrastructure changes task terraform:destroy Tear down infrastructure task terraform:clean Clean up Terraform state

docs.title: Packer Tasks
docs.text: Command Description task packer:build Build VM templates

docs.title: Other
docs.text: Command Description task tui Launch the homelab management TUI

docs.title: TUI
docs.text: A terminal-based management interface for the homelab built with Textual.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The TUI provides an interactive dashboard for managing homelab operations without memorizing CLI commands.

docs.title: Features
docs.text:
• VM management dashboard
  • Ansible deployment interface
  • Command output viewer
  • Service status overview

docs.title: Usage
docs.text: task tui

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
• Python 3.11+
  • Dependencies managed with uv (see tui/pyproject.toml)
